prompt: A listing broker's primary fiduciary duty to the seller client is to:

options.text: Promote the seller's interests with loyalty, care, and obedience within the law
Obtain the highest possible price regardless of the seller's instructions
Represent both parties equally without disclosure
Guarantee a sale within 30 days

explanation: Agency duties center on loyalty and obedience to lawful client instructions, not guaranteeing outcomes.

distractorExplanations.b: Price goals must still follow the client's lawful instructions and market reality.
distractorExplanations.c: Undisclosed dual representation breaches disclosure duties.

prompt: When must a licensee typically disclose a dual agency or designated agency relationship?

options.text: Only after closing
Before or as soon as practicable when the dual/designated relationship arises, per applicable rules
Never, if both parties are customers
Only if the buyer asks in writing after offer acceptance

explanation: Agency disclosure timing is early—when the dual or designated relationship is created or contemplated.

distractorExplanations.c: Customer status does not erase disclosure duties when agency shifts.
distractorExplanations.d: Disclosure is not optional after acceptance.

prompt: An exclusive right-to-sell listing generally means:

options.text: The seller may avoid paying a commission if they find the buyer themselves, in every case
Only the buyer broker can be paid
The listing broker is owed a commission if the property sells during the term, regardless of who finds the buyer (per the agreement)
The listing is open to all brokers without a commission agreement

explanation: Exclusive right-to-sell typically protects the listing broker's commission if a sale occurs during the listing term.

distractorExplanations.a: That is more like exclusive agency or open listing variations.

prompt: Earnest money is best described as:

options.text: A nonrefundable gift to the listing agent personally
The broker's guaranteed commission
A municipal transfer tax paid at listing
A good-faith deposit typically held in escrow/trust pending contract performance

explanation: Earnest money shows good faith and is usually escrowed subject to the contract.

distractorExplanations.a: It is not a personal gift to the agent.
distractorExplanations.c: Transfer taxes are closing items, not earnest money.

prompt: A material fact that must typically be disclosed to a buyer includes:

options.text: Known latent defects or conditions that could significantly affect value or desirability, as required by law
Only the seller's preferred paint color
Confidential seller motivations that the seller forbids disclosing and that are not legally required
Rumors with no known factual basis that the licensee invents

explanation: Material property defects and legally required disclosures must be shared; confidential motivations may be protected.

distractorExplanations.b: Cosmetic preferences are not material defects.
distractorExplanations.c: Confidential client info is protected unless disclosure is required.

prompt: If a buyer customer asks a listing agent for advice on how much to offer, the listing agent should:

options.text: Coach the buyer against the seller's interests without disclosure
Avoid acting as the buyer's advocate and stay within the listing agency role (or properly create buyer agency if allowed)
Always refuse to speak to any buyer
Rewrite the listing price secretly

explanation: Listing agents represent the seller; advocacy for a buyer requires a proper agency shift/disclosure.

distractorExplanations.c: Communication with buyers is normal; advocacy is the issue.

prompt: A contract is generally voidable by a party who:

options.text: Signed while legally competent and fully informed
Paid earnest money
Was induced by fraud or was a minor (depending on circumstances)
Received a copy of the listing agreement

explanation: Fraud or lack of capacity can make a contract voidable by the protected party.

distractorExplanations.b: Earnest money alone does not create voidability.
distractorExplanations.d: Receiving a listing copy is unrelated.

prompt: "Time is of the essence" in a purchase contract typically means:

options.text: Deadlines are merely suggestions
Only the closing date can be ignored
Inspection periods never expire
Contract dates and times are critical and late performance may be a breach

explanation: The clause elevates punctual performance; missing deadlines can be material.

distractorExplanations.b: Other dates can also be critical.

prompt: A net listing arrangement is often risky or restricted because:

options.text: The broker's compensation is the amount above a seller's net price, creating conflict-of-interest concerns
It always increases the buyer's loan amount
It eliminates all disclosure duties
It is required by RESPA for every sale

explanation: Net listings can incentivize underpricing relative to the seller's interests and are banned or tightly regulated in many places.

distractorExplanations.d: RESPA does not require net listings.

prompt: An option contract in real estate gives the optionee:

options.text: An obligation to buy
A right to purchase on stated terms within a period, usually for consideration
Ownership immediately without closing
A free listing cancellation right forever

explanation: An option is a purchased right, not an obligation, to buy on fixed terms for a time.

distractorExplanations.a: That describes a purchase contract obligation.

prompt: Puffing in advertising is generally:

options.text: Fraudulent misstatement of a measurable fact
Always illegal in every jurisdiction without exception
Exaggerated opinion that a reasonable person would not treat as a factual warranty
A required RESPA disclosure form

explanation: Puffing is opinion/hype; misrepresenting material facts is not puffing.

distractorExplanations.b: Overstated—puffing is often distinguished from fraud.

prompt: A licensee who fills in blanks on a standard purchase form for a client is typically:

options.text: Always acting as a judge
Exempt from all contract rules
Required to notarize every page personally
Practicing law if they draft novel legal clauses beyond authorized forms

explanation: Using approved forms is common; drafting custom legal language can be unauthorized practice of law.

distractorExplanations.c: Notarization rules vary and are not universal page-by-page duties.

prompt: If a seller refuses to consent to a disclosed dual agency where required, the broker should:

options.text: Not create the dual agency relationship; consider designated agency, referral, or withdrawal as appropriate
Proceed secretly anyway
Force the seller to sign under threat of lawsuit by the broker
Destroy the listing agreement

explanation: Consent is required for dual agency where the model is used; without it, other compliant paths are needed.

distractorExplanations.b: Secret dual agency is a serious violation.

prompt: A buyer brokerage agreement typically establishes that the buyer broker's client is:

options.text: The listing broker
The buyer
The title company
The lender exclusively

explanation: Buyer agency agreements create fiduciary duties to the buyer client.

distractorExplanations.a: Listing broker is a cooperating party, not the buyer client.
distractorExplanations.c: Title company is a service provider.

prompt: Commingling trust funds means:

options.text: Depositing earnest money into a proper escrow/trust account
Disbursing funds per a written release agreement
Mixing client trust money with personal or operating funds improperly
Keeping detailed escrow ledgers

explanation: Commingling is improper mixing of trust and personal/operating money.

distractorExplanations.a: Proper escrow deposit is required practice.

prompt: A fee simple absolute estate is best characterized as:

options.text: A leasehold for one month
An easement only
A personal property license
The most complete ownership interest recognized in real property

explanation: Fee simple absolute is the fullest freehold estate.

prompt: Which deed offers the greatest protection to a grantee from the grantor's covenants?

options.text: General warranty deed
Quitclaim deed
Bargain and sale deed without covenants
Deed in lieu with no warranties

explanation: General warranty deeds typically include the broadest covenants of title.

distractorExplanations.b: Quitclaim transfers whatever interest the grantor has, often without warranties.
distractorExplanations.d: No warranties implied by that description.

prompt: An easement appurtenant typically:

options.text: Is always personal to one individual and never runs with land
Benefits a dominant tenement and burdens a servient tenement
Transfers only livestock grazing rights federally
Is identical to a fee simple defeasible

explanation: Appurtenant easements run with the dominant estate and burden the servient land.

distractorExplanations.a: That describes many easements in gross more closely.

prompt: A lien that is specific to one property (not all of a debtor's assets) is called a:

options.text: General lien
Homestead exemption
Specific lien
Fee tail

explanation: Specific liens attach to a particular property (e.g., mortgage, property tax lien).

distractorExplanations.a: General liens can reach multiple assets.

prompt: Joint tenancy traditionally includes the right of:

options.text: Dower only
Eminent domain by co-tenants
Automatic conversion to a life estate
Survivorship

explanation: Classic joint tenancy features right of survivorship among joint tenants.

distractorExplanations.a: Dower is a marital interest concept.

prompt: A metes-and-bounds description starts at a:

options.text: Point of beginning
Township number only
Lot number in a condo declaration only
Assessor parcel map without courses

explanation: Metes and bounds walks courses and distances from a point of beginning.

distractorExplanations.c: Lot/block is a different description system.
distractorExplanations.d: APNs identify parcels but aren't metes-and-bounds courses.

prompt: Police power in land-use control includes:

options.text: Only private deed restrictions
Zoning and building codes
Only mortgage foreclosure
Only federal income tax liens

explanation: Zoning and building codes are classic police-power land-use tools.

prompt: Eminent domain requires:

options.text: A private neighbor's consent only
No compensation if the owner objects
Public use/purpose and just compensation (constitutional framework)
A quitclaim from the assessor

explanation: Takings require public purpose and just compensation under the Constitution.

prompt: A life estate ends:

options.text: Never
Only when property taxes are paid
Automatically after 99 years in every state
Upon the death of the measuring life (or as otherwise defined)

explanation: Life estates are measured by a life; remainder/reversion follows.

distractorExplanations.b: Taxes don't define the estate end.

prompt: Encroachments are typically discovered by:

options.text: A survey
A credit report
An appraisal form alone without fieldwork
A listing photo filter

explanation: Surveys reveal boundary improvements that encroach.

distractorExplanations.b: Credit reports don't show physical encroachments.
distractorExplanations.c: Appraisals may note issues but surveys define boundaries.

prompt: A cloud on title is:

options.text: Clear marketable title with no issues
A claim, encumbrance, or defect that may impair marketability
A weather disclosure
A type of zoning variance

explanation: Clouds cast doubt on title and may need quiet-title or curative action.

distractorExplanations.d: Zoning is land use, not title cloud.

prompt: Tenancy in common differs from joint tenancy mainly because tenants in common:

options.text: Always have right of survivorship
Cannot convey their interest
May hold unequal shares and typically lack survivorship unless specified
Must be married

explanation: TIC allows unequal interests and no automatic survivorship.

prompt: A variance in zoning is typically a:

options.text: Permanent change of the zoning map for an entire district
Federal tax credit
Type of easement by necessity
Permission to deviate from a zoning requirement due to hardship, granted by the zoning authority

explanation: Variances grant relief from strict zoning application in hardship cases.

prompt: Constructive notice of a recorded deed is best achieved by:

options.text: Recording the deed in the public land records
Telling only one neighbor verbally
Keeping the deed in a desk drawer forever
Emailing a friend without recording

explanation: Recording provides constructive notice to the world.

distractorExplanations.b: Actual notice to one person is limited.

prompt: Riparian rights generally relate to:

options.text: Only mineral rights under federal leases
Land bordering flowing water such as a river or stream
Air rights above 60,000 feet exclusively
Stock in a REIT

explanation: Riparian doctrines address rights of owners along flowing waters (vs littoral for lakes/oceans).

distractorExplanations.c: Air rights exist but aren't riparian.

prompt: LTV (loan-to-value) is calculated as:

options.text: Monthly payment ÷ annual income
Down payment ÷ interest rate
Loan amount ÷ appraised value (or purchase price, per lender rules) × 100%
Closing costs ÷ HOA dues

explanation: LTV ratios loan size to property value.

prompt: One discount point typically costs:

options.text: 1% of the purchase price always, even if loan is smaller
$1 flat
10% of the down payment
1% of the loan amount

explanation: A point is generally 1% of the loan amount, paid to buy down rate or as lender fees.

distractorExplanations.a: Based on loan, not always price.

prompt: PITI commonly refers to:

options.text: Principal, interest, taxes, and insurance
Points, incentives, title, and inventory
Prepayment, inflation, trusts, and income
Property, improvements, tenants, and improvements again

explanation: PITI is the classic housing payment package lenders underwrite.

prompt: An amortizing fixed-rate mortgage payment primarily covers:

options.text: Interest only forever with no principal reduction
Both interest and principal so the balance declines over the term
Only property taxes
Only HOA capital reserves

explanation: Amortizing payments reduce principal over time while paying interest.

prompt: RESPA's core consumer-protection theme includes:

options.text: Setting property tax rates statewide
Licensing all appraisers internationally
Banning certain kickbacks and requiring settlement cost disclosures in covered transactions
Guaranteeing appraisal values

explanation: RESPA addresses settlement services, kickbacks, and related disclosures (with TRID integration for many loans).

prompt: A conventional loan that is not insured/guaranteed by FHA/VA/USDA is typically called:

options.text: A government loan
A reverse bridge grant
A hard-money gift letter
A conventional loan

explanation: Conventional loans lack FHA/VA/USDA insurance/guarantee.

distractorExplanations.c: Gift letters document gifts; hard money is private lending.

prompt: If annual property taxes are $3,600 and the seller owned the property for 3 months of the tax year at closing (buyer pays full year later), a typical proration if seller owes buyer for prepaid/advance assumptions depends on custom—but the monthly tax amount is:
explanation: $3,600 ÷ 12 = $300 per month—the unit used in many proration setups.

prompt: A prepayment penalty clause:

options.text: Forbids the lender from charging interest
Requires extra payment if the borrower pays off early, when the note allows it
Is identical to PMI
Always applies to every VA loan by federal mandate

explanation: Prepayment penalties charge for early payoff when permitted by the loan terms and law.

distractorExplanations.d: VA loans generally restrict such penalties.

prompt: Private mortgage insurance (PMI) is most often required when:

options.text: The buyer pays all cash
The loan is a HELOC interest-only forever by definition
Conventional LTV is high (commonly above 80%)
The property is vacant land with no lender

explanation: PMI protects conventional lenders when down payment/equity is thin.

prompt: An adjustable-rate mortgage (ARM) interest rate:

options.text: Never changes
Is set only by the listing broker
Must equal the prime rate minus 10% by law
Can adjust based on an index plus margin, subject to caps

explanation: ARMs adjust with index + margin within contractual caps.

prompt: Title insurance primarily protects against:

options.text: Covered title defects and claims against ownership per the policy
Fire and flood damage to the structure
Borrower default on the note
HOA special assessments always without exception

explanation: Owner's/lender's title policies insure against covered title risks, not casualty.

prompt: A debit to the buyer on a closing statement means:

options.text: The buyer receives a credit reducing cash to close
The buyer is charged; it increases cash needed (or reduces proceeds if seller)
The item is always illegal
The lender pays the item automatically

explanation: Debits charge a party; buyer debits typically increase funds due at closing.

prompt: The secondary mortgage market's main function is to:

options.text: Originate every purchase loan face-to-face only
Set local property tax millage
Buy and sell existing mortgages, improving liquidity for originators
Issue building permits

explanation: Entities like Fannie/Freddie (and others) provide liquidity by purchasing loans.

prompt: Usury laws generally regulate:

options.text: Maximum lot sizes in zoning
Minimum square footage of garages
Only HOA pet policies
Maximum interest rates that may be charged on certain loans

explanation: Usury caps address excessive interest.

prompt: A bridge loan is typically used to:

options.text: Finance a short-term gap, often between purchase of a new home and sale of an existing one
Fund a 30-year fully amortizing primary residence as the only permanent loan forever
Pay property taxes for a municipality
Replace title insurance

explanation: Bridge (swing) financing covers short gaps in housing transitions.

prompt: When a Idaho licensee changes brokers, they typically must:

options.text: Keep practicing under both brokers secretly without notice
Notify {c} / follow transfer procedures so the license is properly affiliated
Destroy their license wall certificate and continue unlicensed
Wait 20 years before transferring

explanation: Affiliation changes require regulatory notice/transfer steps.

prompt: Document retention for Idaho brokerages typically requires:

options.text: Shredding all files the day after closing always
Storing records only in a personal phone with no backup forever
Keeping transaction records for the period required by {c}
Never retaining trust account records

explanation: Statutory retention periods protect consumers and enable audits.

prompt: Guaranteeing a future profit to a Idaho buyer as a direct promise by a licensee is:

options.text: Required by every listing agreement
The definition of an appraisal
Mandated by RESPA Form A
Generally improper/misleading and can be a license law violation

explanation: Profit guarantees are deceptive; licensees must avoid false assurances.

distractorExplanations.b: Appraisals estimate value, they don't guarantee investor profits.

prompt: Presenting a written offer to a Idaho seller client should generally occur:

options.text: Promptly as required by agency and license rules
Only after the listing expires
Never if the offer is below list price
Only verbally with no written record

explanation: Licensees must timely present offers unless the client gave lawful instructions otherwise.

distractorExplanations.c: Low offers still must be presented.

prompt: Handling of transaction files after a Idaho associate leaves a firm typically:

options.text: Allows the associate to take all trust funds personally
Remains with the brokerage; the firm retains responsibility for records
Deletes the broker's duty to supervise past deals
Transfers escrow money to the associate's rent account

explanation: Brokerages own/control transaction records and remaining trust obligations.

distractorExplanations.a: Trust funds stay in proper accounts.

prompt: Trust account reconciliations for a Idaho brokerage are important because:

options.text: They replace the need for licenses
They set the local MLS dues
They help detect shortages/overages and demonstrate compliance to {c}
They determine property tax assessments

explanation: Regular reconciliation is a core trust-account control.

prompt: If Idaho Real Estate Commission investigates a complaint against a Idaho licensee, the licensee should:

options.text: Ignore all notices
Alter transaction files to hide issues
Threaten the complainant with unlicensed practice
Cooperate as required and respond truthfully within deadlines

explanation: Regulatory cooperation and honesty are mandatory; obstruction worsens outcomes.

prompt: Discriminatory refusal to show a Idaho home based on a protected class under fair housing law is:

options.text: Illegal under federal fair housing law and typically also violates {s} license standards
Required for exclusive listings
Allowed if the seller asks orally
Encouraged in team meetings

explanation: Fair housing prohibitions override discriminatory seller instructions.

prompt: A Idaho associated licensee wants to run personal real estate ads that omit the supervising broker. This is generally:

options.text: Best practice for branding
Noncompliant with brokerage identification rules
Required for teams
Only allowed on billboards

explanation: Ads usually must name the responsible broker/firm.

prompt: A temporary/practice permit or provisional status in Idaho, where offered, generally means:

options.text: Unlimited practice rights worldwide
No supervision is ever allowed
Limited authority under {c} rules until full licensing conditions are met
The person is exempt from all advertising rules forever

explanation: Provisional statuses are narrower and conditioned.

prompt: A Idaho licensee acting as a dual agent must typically:

options.text: Push the buyer to overpay to favor the seller secretly
Share each party's bottom line freely without consent
Ignore disclosure forms
Remain neutral on price advocacy and keep confidential client information as required

explanation: Dual agents cannot favor one client and must protect confidential info within the rules.

distractorExplanations.b: Bottom lines are confidential without consent.

prompt: A Idaho licensee wants to advertise "We sell homes 20% above market every time." This claim is:

options.text: Likely deceptive unless substantiated and carefully qualified
Always safe puffing
Required by MLS
A substitute for a CMA

explanation: Guaranteed market-beating claims are high-risk advertising.

distractorExplanations.d: CMAs analyze value; ads aren't CMAs.

prompt: A Idaho licensee receives a referral from another state. Best practice is to:

options.text: Pay any unlicensed finder any amount in cash without records
Confirm licensure status and follow {c} rules on referral fees and advertising
Ignore whether the referrer is licensed
Split trust deposits with the referrer personally

explanation: Interstate referrals still require compliant compensation and licensing checks.

prompt: Operating without the required Idaho real estate license while performing brokerage for others for compensation is:

options.text: Encouraged for new agents before class
Allowed on weekends only
Unlicensed practice that {c} can stop with penalties
Legal if the person uses the word "consultant"

explanation: Labels don't legalize unlicensed brokerage.

prompt: False or misleading advertising by a Idaho licensee can result in:

options.text: Automatic immunity forever
A required bonus commission from the state
Exemption from all license laws
Disciplinary action by Idaho Real Estate Commission

explanation: Idaho Real Estate Commission can discipline licensees for deceptive ads.

distractorExplanations.b: States do not pay bonus commissions for violations.
